title: 2025/09/11 - TI
displayTitle: 2025-09-11 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Inquiry into ports and the maritime sector
topic: Inquiry into ports and the maritime sector
description: 11 September 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Inquiry into ports and the maritime sector New Zealand Port CEO Group Charles Finn,y Interim Chair
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into ports and the maritime sector. New Zealand Port CEO Group. Charles Finn,y Interim Chair

chunks.text: Kick off with a few, few comments, and then we'll love to, uh, ask you lots of Questions. Great. Well, that's really good. 'cause normally one gets 10 minutes. Mm-hmm. And, and you get two or three questions if you are, if you're lucky. So it's good quality time, Duration until about, uh, five to Oh, No, that's really wonderful. So, um, I guess the first point, so I'm, I'm speaking on Charles Ney, um, independent chair of the, uh, New Zealand Pork Company, CEO group.

Um, and, um, first point to make is, uh, to say thank you for doing this inquiry, because anything that, um, puts the spotlight on the supply chain, in particular, the port sector, um, is, is very welcome. 'cause often we are, forget, forgotten.

Often we are seen as sort of a poor cousin to aviation and, and, and other parts of the sector. But as you all know, 'cause I know you are really working hard and you're visiting lots of ports, um, uh, this is a sector that, that is critically important to the economy. Um, we, we can't survive without exporting. Uh, and 99% of our exports by volume are going through the seaports.

And we can't survive, as we found out during COVID without quite a lot of importing as well. And, and, uh, most of that product comes through the seaports as well. So we want to have them operating as, um, efficiently as possible. Uh, and, um, uh, government, um, can play a very important role, uh, in terms of us getting there.

Most, um, most significant significantly from our perspective. Um, uh, government can help with planning, uh, processes and laws. Um, and we have been, uh, supportive of the reforms to the RMA that, um, uh, both the labor led government and, and this government, um, have been progressing and we're grateful, uh, for the wide support in Parliament.

Um, for, for the, to be reformed, the most obvious problem, and I'm sure you visited the port of Toran or you're going to, but, but, uh, the most obvious problem is, is, is that consent, which has been going on for seven years or so, costing, uh, tens of millions of dollars. And, uh, um, um, basically stopping the expansion of our, our major export port.

And, uh, we're in the situation right now where our major export port is having to turn business away because it's at, at, um, at capacity. Um, uh, but we have a number of other ports that that, that, um, have projects, uh, pending, uh, thanks to the fast track process.

Port of Auckland has got their, their major project through, um, we've got, uh, Northport wanting to expand, um, and hasn't been able to get its consent finalized. Uh, we've got, um, thanks to earthquakes, no, no consenting issue for, for, for the, uh, Littleton Port Company, but they've got a, they need money to, um, for an expansion there, they're ready to go.

Uh, and there's a major, um, inland port project that, uh, port Otago is working on, and pretty much every port has got, um, a project there. Uh, and we need to have, uh, uh, uh, an efficient and quick way to get the consents. Um, we, um, uh, have, um, highlighted in our submission, um, uh, the, um, uh, issues around, um, uh, the, uh, ownership reports.

Um, and we were pleased that you raised this in the, uh, terms of reference, um, uh, for this, um, uh, inquiry. Um, uh, we have a range of ownership models, uh, for, for the port sector. Um, uh, but one thing that is consistent throughout, uh, this, the, this, uh, port system is, uh, ownership by regional councils or their equivalent.

Um, and, um, we don't have a, uh, uniform view on whether that's a good or a bad thing, but very happy for, have you having you debate, debate that. Um, I do note that, um, some of those ports that, um, have got some private sector ownership, um, normally it's 33%, um, private sector ownership, uh, are open to far more scrutiny, um, from the analysts than the other ports.

And, and that, uh, you could argue is, uh, is assisting the performance of those courts. Um, this is a really interesting time for you to be doing, uh, doing the study because things have been sort of pretty consistent for the last 10, 15 years. Not major change to the sector, but you have the feeling that there is major change gonna gonna happen over the next five to 10 years.

Um, if we are able to get the Taronga consent, uh, through, you're gonna see, uh, after the construction there a, um, very automated, um, port operation, which will have an immediate, um, impact on, um, on productivity. Um, the Littleton project likewise is gonna involve an element of, um, of automation that likewise would help lift, uh, productivity.

And I think the North Port project, um, will be, uh, seeing some automation as well. And that will help productivity there. Other ports are gonna have to respond 'cause it is a competitive model, which is a very good thing. Um, internationally, uh, we are, um, subject to, um, um, uh, a range of, of, um, uh, regulation.

Um, most interestingly, um, there is work being done on a, um, a potential, um, uh, climate change, um, related, um, uh, charge, uh, on, on some fuels. And in response to that, there is enormous, um, work being, uh, put into, um, new fuel sources for the international shipping fleet.

Um, some of, um, the ports are very interested in this and that they see New Zealand as a potential producer of some of these, um, alternative fuels. Um, but certainly we, we, we are not going to be able to sit here and ignore what is um, uh, agreed internationally and we'll have to respond.

Um, we're also seeing a trend towards, um, bigger ships. I imagine that will, will continue. But, uh, like everything, um, we are being disrupted by the trade policy of the United States. And also, uh, some of the environmental policies, uh, being pursued by the United States.

Uh, the International Maritime Organization was very close to achieving a, uh, agreed policy. Uh, United States walked out of the room, uh, and, um, uh, latest I've seen the United States as threatening those who might, um, actually implement the likely policy with some form of retaliation.

So again, we are going to have to, um, um, take note of, of, of what is happening there. I think the final point, um, uh, to make is that, um, uh, the reason perhaps why we weren't focusing enough on ports was that up until, uh, COVID striking the port sector was, was just doing its job, doing the job, uh, very effectively.

Um, and then we had a major disruption, and we haven't recovered from that. I don't think you can say that it's COVID that is causing the current disruptions. But we have, um, had drought and Panama Canal, we've had, um, uh, we have the Houthis, um, uh, attacking shipping in the Red, red Sea.

Um, and, um, we've had, uh, huge surges of trade, uh, as people are trying to get their product, um, uh, into the United States ahead of, um, proposed tariff increases. And then we've had diversion of trade, um, because of, uh, tariff policy in the United States, all of which, um, is impacting on the reliability of shipping to New Zealand.

We still haven't got reliability, um, um, and, and ships sticking to window and the way we had, uh, prior to COVID. And this is one of the factors at play when we talk about the productivity challenge. So why don't I leave that things there. Very happy to take your questions. Um, and thank you again for the work you're doing.

I'm not sure I'm aware of a select committee that has done so much groundwork, uh, and, and study of our sector. So thank you very much. Thank You. Thank you. It's really appreciate that. Um, look, I've got Dan start off with then, and then I've, I'll kick in and then Julian and camp. Thanks Charles. Great submission. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Um, just wanna dive deeper 'cause I think you've, you've touched on all the key things.

I think this is your opportunity to tell us what you think the changes need to be in order to get more productivity and efficiency and effectiveness from the sector. So you talked about the fact that you're pleased that we've got regulation, governance, and ownership reports on the table. My question to you is, what do you think we need to be looking at, uh, and recommending to the sector or to government, um, to improve the productivity or the organization of the sector?

One of the challenges of the role I perform as the independent chair is trying to reflect a diverse, um, range of, of, of views. And I can't say there's an absolute consensus for them, the in in the group, but I think if, um, uh, speaking hypothetically, I would be, um, asking questions about whether this is the best ownership model for, for the port sector.

Um, ask what would change if perhaps there was, um, a required, um, sell down of, of say 33% to, um, the equity market, for example. Um, and, um, what this would mean in terms of, um, port operation, but also, um, what it would mean in terms of possible consolidation within the sector.

So you, you'd recommend that, um, you'd recommend that in terms of, uh, ownership that we look at more private capital or ownership of Lina? Yeah, well our, our local is our regional councils the best owner would be my question. Um, have you got any examples of regional councils as shareholders contributing new funds for major infrastructure, uh, investment at ports? I ask the question.

Yeah. Has anybody got any subs on that particular issue of ownership? Yeah, I do. Brilliant. Um, 'cause one thing that is interesting to me about this is, is two things. Well, there's two, sorry. There's two things, not one thing. Um, for, um, supply chain and freight transport efficiency for the country, country as a whole, it's not just the individual decisions that affect the productivity of an in of one port.

There's a bunch of ways in which it influences landside infrastructure that would be provided by central and local government. Or if we were to be able to support more coastal shipping, uh, and the hub and spoke spoke model, we could avoid a whole bunch of costs potentially if we had more frequency and efficient options there, like a avoid road costs and road transport costs.

So, um, I'm wondering how, like, in that sense, how does, how do we, how would private ownership help New Zealand to make those strategic decisions about investment and about which ports grow and get more investment, um, versus, you know, whereas if it's privately owned and they're just all focusing on their own operations and not considering the NZ ink perspective.

But then my other question is, obviously this has happened with regional or well unitary authority, um, ownership, but in Auckland, um, you know, prices are going up and that's affecting cargo owners and, and, um, transport costs. But why wouldn't private owners also seek to maximize their return from that as well?

Um, well that, that's, you, you sort of answered the question. Um, I think it's a neutral answer, um, that the, the ports right now are required, even though they might be owned a hundred percent by a unitary council or regional council or a couple of regional councils, um, are required to operate like private companies and, and, and, and maximize, uh, the return.

So, so, so that, that, that would be the situation regardless of whether there was private money or, or, or whether we stuck with the status quo. Um, but I think the point you're making is that this is, that this is a, um, a system Yeah. That, that, that, um, uh, operates.

Um, so a ship will call probably, well, when we're talking about containers, okay, a container ship will probably call it multiple ports around New Zealand. And if one port is slow, that will have a systemic, um, impact. If there's some weather re disruption, there will be an impact.

The, the system will only work well if everyone is efficient. And that should be our goal. But your point is, well, I guess it is a hundred percent correct that this is a system and what solving the problem for one port is not gonna solve the problem for New Zealand. Well, I guess how do we, I'm to talk about, I'll let Julia, I'm happy to talk about hub and spoke maybe as a separate company.

We'll talk that as a separate topic. Okay. Can Just real quick. So you, sorry, um, a question, uh, Julian, Jen has just asked about, um, efficiency and thinking about New Zealand Inc as a whole. Uh, you've talked about the efficiency for, you know, the, the, the matter, the ownership model, it might be the same, but the question is, have you seen, uh, unitary or regional council owned ports take a mindset that is more New Zealand Inc than private ports, private owned ports, private equity?

I think there is, um, over time there's been evidence of parochialism in some decision making. No. And, and ownership structure hasn't provided that you've been able to observe. Yeah, I think, I think though, um, you'd have to say that some of the larger ports have to by definition because they're, they are feed taking the, the freight from a wider, um, hinterland.

Um, and it might be all of New Zealand in some cases are having to think systemically, maybe some other ports less, It's not the ownership the size is what, Yeah. So, on on ownership to wrap that one there, two things. The first one of 'em is, are you seeing any evidence that there is a, um, a shortage of capital which relates to the, uh, the ownership model? I mean, I can can imagine Hundred percent Littleton, Littleton might be an Issue a hundred percent on Littleton. Yeah. Yep.

Yeah. Littleton will be the obvious one, I would think. Yeah. Um, the rest no evidence, no. So just wondering whether in the sense of an ownership, if there were a requirement to sell down or to, um, to issue new equity that that, that, that is in the context of the need to invest in the port itself. So an example might again, be North port's got a pretty significant, um, investment proposition. Is that what you're talking about?

Well, I think North port's a slightly different case because don't forget it's 50% owned by, by, uh, to Yes. Yeah. And, uh, so they, they, but there is a Regional council Element there as well. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So my question really is if there is a need to invest in the ports, is that being held up by the ownership, leaving aside Littleton?

And is the, is the, the, if you said that was the requirement to, if the, if, if for example, the proposal hypothetically just to say, well, you need to sell down, it's in the context of needing to sell down to invest in something in the port as opposed to simply sell down some shares for freeing up capital for regional councils, which goodness only knows what they do with, uh, The, the ports could, um, look at a range of funding vehicles for, for capital expansion.

Okay. And the other area in terms of ports, some of the ports appointed at other ports and said that becau you, you raised that issue of transparency, that, um, they're not being particularly transparent with their operation and therefore some parts their operation effectively are subsidized some of their others in what should be a competitive space, which maybe they shouldn't be doing what they're doing.

You got any observations on that? Well, without doubt, if you are, um, uh, listed, you are having to, um, meet a higher standard of transparency than if you are not. And, and, um, I commend to you, if you haven't already looked at it, the most recent, um, uh, result, uh, and the associated documents from port toga, it's all there.

Yeah. Okay. The, the 50% on ation. Yeah. But more than 50%. Yeah. But it is, um, yeah, I, I, I should, should have also stressed in my introductory comments that, um, we tend to talk about container trade. Yep. Yes. That's 50% of the equation. Yeah.

Um, and bulk is hugely important for New Zealand. So in terms of exports, the most obvious area is logs, but they're very big, uh, animal feed, um, imports, very, very big fertilizer imports. Um, we've also got, um, uh, a lot of fuel coming Okay.

Into, into New Zealand. So, so, um, when we're talking about productivity, um, um, in, in this context, we should probably distinguish between the two because, um, a lot of the, um, uh, negative comment is coming about the container Yeah. Trade, but, but in terms of the bulk trade, we are a very productive board sector.

Okay. That's, that's useful. No, and it's a different model too. You, you basically, you're dealing with a ship going point to point. Yeah. And not, not subject to the same delays you get around the coast, that container ship. That's good. Sorry. No, that's good to have, Tanya. You wanna get, take some different, I think. Yes, I do think you, Mr. Chair. Thanks Charles. Just on the infrastructure investment piece, your submission sort of touches on enhancement for dry dock facilities now.

Um, and it's just one sentence, which is fine, but we hear a lot about, uh, Northport being, you know, the, the option there. Does the group have any, any views around what enhancement would look like? I mean, we might assume what that looks like, but wanna hear from you directly? Um, I think the, the group would definitely welcome, um, um, a a couple of projects that perhaps could be, uh, assisted by government.

Um, uh, having a, we actually do have a couple of dry docks in New Zealand, but they're not of sufficient size to, to, um, cope with the ferries or, or larger ships. Uh, so having a large dry dock in New Zealand would, would certainly be something that would, would, would be, um, uh, something that could potentially enhance, um, uh, efficiency, productivity, uh, around, around the coast.

Um, but you'd have to make sure you got your, your numbers right in terms of working, whether you get a proper return on that, that capital, uh, investment. But, you know, when I was a, uh, a young person, I remember going fishing in Wellington Harbor and, and actually fishing off the dry dock that used to be there. Uh, so we've had them in the past and, and they were there for a reason.

Um, and, um, uh, uh, I personally would like to see, see, see, um, uh, one somewhere. Um, um, but there's also the possibility of some, um, and we're working with Maritime New Zealand, um, and, and the wider government system on this, um, a, a, um, uh, a vessel, uh, or vessels able to help ships in distress and make sure they, they don't wash onto the rocks and things like that.

So, so, so having a, um, uh, a heavy toe, uh, capacity in New Zealand would be something that, that would be welcome as well. Thanks. Did you wanna carry, you wanna carry on this? I think, uh, d workforce and schools development. Okay. Maybe come back to that just, just just on what, um, Charles has just raised, because we have actually had the conversation around, um, those, uh, safety, safety vessels, heavy haulage ability.

Um, every port that I've talked to, mostly of the ones in this part of the world sort of said, well, yeah, we think it's really, really important, but it's not our business 'cause we can't get a return on it somebody else's. Can you just walk us through what we've, you know, we've obviously had ferries getting, coming, coming to grief occasionally, and, um, or losing power. We had the, um, type, what was it, the, um, container vessel freight or whatever it was that, uh, was got itself into trouble.

What's your view of how that is best resolved? Well, We've been very lucky, um, having, um, uh, based at Port Taranaki a number of vessels with the, uh, capability. We're talking about, um, servicing the offshore oil industry and gas industry.

So, um, we've been able to, um, essentially get, get those vessels to, uh, to divert and provided the assistance necessary. Uh, in terms of, um, a couple of recent, uh, instances, uh, and another, um, the Wellington, uh, uh, tugs were able to get out there, um, and do something they're probably not designed to do.

They're held it up. That's about all The control, I think. Yeah, yeah, exactly. Um, so this is something we, we, we should be thinking about. And the problem is though, um, no individual 'cause ports had to be operating as companies and, and, um, uh, not, not, uh, there to provide public goods of this type.

Um, uh, it's very difficult to justify the case, uh, for a, a tug that is, is large enough to provide the, uh, the towing, um, uh, capability. Um, um, um, you that that'd be most, most ports will, will, will have, um, um, a a desire to have, uh, such a service within a reasonable range, but they're not really the people that should be funding it.

So who are the people we, I think a joint venture with government would be, or, or, um, the wider, the wider industry. And, and you know, as you are aware, maritime New Zealand is funded through a levy, maybe it's could be built into the levy levy, uh, calculation and due course. That's helpful. Right. I think the next one I hope is Julianne. Um, Yeah, actually I kind of somewhat related to what I was asking earlier, but mostly focusing on paragraph 10 and 11 of your submission.

I was just, you know, there is that point that, um, the whole transport system, road rail Yeah. And coastal shipping and international shipping are all a network and therefore, uh, the responsibility to some extent of government where most of it is. Um, you mentioned that ideally all road connections to port should be through a state highway that is grade separated from rail.

Um, like how would you see that funded, I guess who, who should be paying for those sort of improvements? Do you see that just as a New Zealand investment, or are the users paying for that? Uh, it would be, um, like funded by NZTA. Right? So not, not the, Uh, and funded using funded funded the way, you know, with the same funding model at NZTA, um, gets funded, which of course is changing, um, um, uh, right now.

Um, but it's, it, given that we're talking about absolutely critical infrastructure of national importance, um, it, it, it seems, um, a little bit strange that the final few kilometers often is the responsibility of a, of a local council as opposed to, um, uh, NZTA, whereas, whereas we're talking about a national network, um, um, of freight.

Um, and so it's, it's, it's like the last kilometer of a railroad line, not being kiwi rail responsible, you know, it, it doesn't really make that much sense. So, um, uh, if, if those roads could be brought within the NZTA family, I think it would allow us to think in the system a wide sense in a way that we're not able to right now, and we can ensure that there is appropriate investment where that investment is needed.

Um, we've been talking a lot about road and rail, and they do need to be as efficient as possible if we're gonna have a fully, um, efficient, um, port sector. But we also need to be talking about coastal shipping. I dunno if you have any questions on that as a separate topic, but Yeah, that I was gonna go there next. In paragraph 11, you're talking about demand for coastal shipping likely to grow. And I see that as a, a huge opportunity really, that isn't currently weighed up by NZTA or in our, uh, transport planning to say, um,

is the best option here to, you know, upgrade one road or is it to look at, um, you know, supporting coastal shipping? 'cause maybe actually somewhat subsidizing the operation of services to be frequent would have greater benefits than, you know, in terms of reduced cost.

Um, and that's something that I just think we need someone to kind of do the math on and figure it out, but no one's currently in a position to do that. And I guess one thing that we're, you know, well, I'm not arguing for regional council ownership ports, but I think if, if they were privately owned, I'm interested in how, how we continue to take a New Zealand Inc kind of perspective on investments and infrastructure as opposed to investors just wanting to maximize their return on, on operations at a single place.

Well, um, I'll ask the last point first. Um, if that was the sole motivation of private, um, uh, investors, I'm not sure to, we be wanting to do this expansion. They, they, they've got, um, a range of customers there who basically have to use that port.

Mm. Um, and uh, they could do very nicely just, but by watching the demand increase and Right, right. And registering prices, they're not doing that. Okay. They're wanting to invest in an expansion because, um, they know that we are as a country going to have increased, um, demand for their services. Um, government is talking about doubling the value of exports by 2034. Uh, we're, It's not necessarily we're Well on doubling the volume.

No, no, it's that it's not. Yeah. But, but, but we know there is gonna be more productivity and, and if we can get the economy growing more quickly, um, we will be seeing a commensurate increase in demand for imports. Uh, so, so, um, uh, I think all the ports are thinking about their role, um, um, as a essential service provider for New Zealand, regardless of their ownership model, but those that are privately owned and maybe are able to respond more quickly to, to, to some of the, um, shifts in demand and finance things more easily.

But, but, um, uh, as we were saying before, I don't think there's a finance or lack of finance is, is, is is the problem, except in perhaps in one, one case, and I think there's no problem with finance there either. It's just a question of how it's prioritized within a region's, uh, list of essential projects.

Um, now, um, on coastal, um, um, it has to grow because we are, we are inevitably going to be moving to more of a hub and spoke model because the ships are gonna be larger and they're not gonna be calling it as many ports, uh, as they were in the past.

And so we will need to have more freight going from port to port by sea, by road, and by rail. Um, I think we'd leave it to the market to, to determine that ideally we can have a more level playing field. 'cause right now, um, uh, anyone wanting to set up a domestic service that's New Zealand flagged is, is facing much increased costs on an international Can I just, can just ask a question, sorry.

She spotted a typo. Um, uh, but this is one of the issues that you've probably talked to coastal providers, um, already in this process. No, we, we will Be though. Okay. Well, this will be one of the things that they say that that, that they have to pay New Zealand wage rates, whereas an international competitor doesn't, um, they, an international competitor doesn't need to pay, um, uh, GST on a lot of inputs and we're subject to the emissions trading scheme, um, uh, for our fuels.

Um, whereas, um, internationally, um, ships will often bunker offshore and not, not pay that, that cost. Now, that will equalize as the international rules kick in, and we, we have, um, an international standard applied, uh, and I'm expecting that most countries will be part of that process, subject of course, to, um, navigating the, um, the language outta Washington, which is making everyone very nervous. Yes, of course. We, Um, subs off this one, 'cause I've got at least three areas that I think I'd like to go.

Um, the first one of those is just, um, it's interesting that when I, I've asked this question about port's, roles and profitability versus their, their role and everyone of the owners that has said, our main, the main function of our ports isn't to make money, it's to do their job in the context of New Zealand d can get any exports and imports in and out, which is, I think it's great. Um, can you just talk about it, um, the, um, you said if, if profitability was the only driver tower on ordinary, be looking to expand the port, you've also said,

and I know this is the case, that they've had to turn, um, business away, they've had to turn a shipping line away, they can't take it. Yeah. Um, this, the issue which they've met, they've said to me is that the whole system is very, very finely tuned. In other words, if something goes wrong, road goes out, it crane falls over, whatever it might, whatever else it might be, the whole system, um, gets impacted that productivity, the reliability, et cetera gets impacted.

How do we deal with that? That, you know, if you've got, if you're just at that tipping point, very, very efficient until something goes wrong, how do you fund essentially the redundant, um, asset to make sure that when something does go wrong, you can actually cope with shipping? You know what I mean? You've actually got some spares there. It's like saying you, you, you four to four tile in the car, that's great.

You don't want the one, don't wanna pay for the fifth one until you get a puncture and then you really need it. Yeah. Well, the system is actually quite resilient. Um, um, and, and we've demonstrated, um, with major earthquakes, with major climatic events, um, um, an ability to, uh, respond within days, um, uh, when, when one port has has closed, but that, that will have an impact around the coast and, and will impact productivity.

But we, we can keep, keep operating. We perhaps aren't as efficient as, uh, we would like to be. But look, the, the, the, the, the main issue for us right now is this on time arrival by international shipping lines. Um, and, um, prior to COVID, the average was 80 to 85% on time arrival from the international shipping lines.

Um, in 2024, things appeared to be getting a little bit better. We were up to 66%. Now it's back down to 55%. Uh, so it's really, really difficult to try and become more productive up against those figures. Um, and, um, in, in terms of, um, uh, port of Auckland, only 60% of container vessels have turned up on time in, in this financial year.

So, um, uh, we, we have forces beyond our control there. Um, having a major impact. Um, we, I think are much better at controlling what is happening around, uh, our coast subject. Of course, the fact that there are storms and other interventions we can't predict.

But, um, uh, when we have the new Port of Tara project through, and that's up and running and automated when we have the new Littleton project through, when we have the new Northport project through, and when we, we have a couple of other minor projects and then port projects through, we will be in a far better position to, to not only be more resilient, but be more efficient.

I think that you're not gonna see a step change until we get that tar on project through. And then that's throughout the, throughout the sector, unfortunately, you'll be aware of the latest development. Yes, I'm sure. So, so, uh, we're, we're, we're, we're, we're not gonna get that consent even considered now till the end of the year, maybe early next year, there's another, you know, another six month delay.
